description: Diagnose the seeded SP revenue drop. Input is the Anomaly object produced by seededToAnomaly(sp-revenue-drop-w4) — same shape the production monitoring agent emits (this is Path C: bypass detection, isolate diagnosis behavior). PR E's diagnosis eval established that this anomaly currently scores 0/10 (0%) on the rubric; the regression check is for STABILITY of that low-rate behavior, not for high rubric scores.

anomaly.impact: revenue for state:SP fell ~30% in a discrete window. Investigate the root cause.

golden_output.conclusion: The reported ~30% revenue decline in SP for week 4 (Dec 22–29) is a baseline distortion artifact caused by an extraordinary electronics-led promotional spike during the week of Dec 8–14, which inflated the 12-week rolling baseline. SP electronics revenue surged from ~R$20.9K/day to a peak of ~R$53.5K/day during Dec 8–14 (a ~10x uplift), then sharply normalized post-promotion. The Dec 22–29 SP revenue of R$32,442 (BRL, normalized) is actually +17% above the true pre-spike weekly average of R$27,750, confirming no genuine demand collapse occurred — the anomaly is a statistical artifact of the elevated baseline.
evidence: SP daily revenue time series shows a massive spike Dec 8–14 (peak R$53,518 on Dec 13, vs ~R$5,400–R$10,000/day in the weeks before and after), then a sharp normalization Dec 15–28.

Electronics revenue in SP spiked from R$20,548 (week of Dec 1) to R$209,252 (week of Dec 8) — a ~10x increase — then collapsed to R$11,825 (week of Dec 15) and R$11,825 (week of Dec 22), making it the primary driver of the spike and the apparent dip.
get_anomaly_context for SP (Dec 22–29 vs 12-week baseline) returned pct_change = +0.169 (+17%), confirming that week 4 SP revenue was actually above the true pre-spike average, not below it.

Credit card payments in SP spiked from R$36,137 (Dec 1 week) to R$167,252 (Dec 8 week), then fell to R$20,021 (Dec 22 week) — tracking electronics demand exactly and ruling out payment disruption as a separate cause.
Vouchers in SP spiked from R$2,624 (Dec 1 week) to R$25,385 (Dec 8 week) then normalized to R$1,968 (Dec 22 week), consistent with a promotional discount event driving the Dec 8 spike.

The spike and normalization pattern is nationwide: RJ went from R$12.3M (Dec 8 wk) to R$3.2M (Dec 22 wk); MG from R$11.8M to R$2.0M; every state followed the same trajectory, ruling out any SP-specific supply or logistics disruption.

Electronics category get_anomaly_context (Dec 22–29 vs Dec 8–15 peak baseline) shows pct_change = -88.8%, with all other categories also declining 21–47%, confirming the spike in Dec 8–14 was the reference distorting the baseline.

hypothesesConsidered.hypothesis: Post-promotional demand normalization / baseline distortion from a Dec 8–14 electronics spike inflating the 12-week baseline
hypothesesConsidered.reasoning: The time series clearly shows SP revenue spiked ~10x during Dec 8–14 (electronics-led, with credit card and voucher payments tracking in lockstep), then reverted to pre-spike levels. The 12-week baseline was heavily skewed by this spike, making the normal post-Christmas week appear as a -30% anomaly. The get_anomaly_context tool using a proper pre-spike baseline shows SP week 4 was actually +17% above average, confirming the baseline distortion hypothesis.

hypothesesConsidered.hypothesis: Payment method disruption (e.g., credit card or boleto failure) blocking SP orders in Dec 22–29
hypothesesConsidered.reasoning: All payment types (credit card, boleto, debit card, voucher) moved proportionally and followed the same spike-then-normalize pattern as every other state. There is no evidence of a single payment type collapsing while others held steady, which would be the signature of a payment disruption.

hypothesesConsidered.hypothesis: SP-specific supply, logistics, or demand shock (e.g., weather, strike, regional event) causing a localized revenue collapse
hypothesesConsidered.reasoning: The normalized revenue pattern in Dec 22–29 is identical across all Brazilian states — RJ, MG, PR, RS, and all others show the same spike in Dec 8–14 and return to baseline in Dec 22–29. If the cause were SP-specific, other states would have maintained elevated revenue during week 4. Instead, the pattern is perfectly correlated nationally, pointing to a shared demand cycle (pre-Christmas promotional pull-forward) rather than any SP-specific disruption.

affectedCustomers.segmentDescription: No customers were adversely affected — week 4 SP revenue was +17% above true pre-spike baseline. The anomaly is a measurement artifact from a distorted 12-week rolling baseline, not a genuine demand loss.

scoring_config.notes: Required fields are the canonical Diagnosis triple from lib/mcp/types.ts. Optional fields (affectedCustomers, confidence, timeSeries) drift between runs and aren't required. The similarity judge anchors on the SUPPORTED HYPOTHESIS — that's where regression matters.
